topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammer against the post felt like an intrusion, a sharp, unwelcome punctuation mark in my otherwise quiet afternoon. From my kitchen window, I watched Mr. Henderson meticulously measure the distance between the new slats. He was always so eager, so relentlessly proactive, as if the very sight of a boundary being defined brought him some profound sense of satisfaction. I kept my back to the glass, focusing instead on the way the sunlight caught the dust motes dancing in the air—a far more interesting spectacle than the impending barrier dividing our properties. Every time he paused to wipe his brow, a tight knot formed low in my stomach, a dull ache that tightened with each passing moment of his construction.

[story 2]

"Are you going to be using that section for the new gate, or just a solid panel?" I asked, the words clipped and flat. My voice carried a brittle edge I couldn't quite suppress. He beamed, a wide, unearned smile that didn't quite reach his eyes. "Oh, just a simple privacy fence, dear. Keeps things tidy." I watched him gesture expansively toward the overgrown hedge bordering my yard, a gesture that seemed to imply I was somehow obstructing his vision. I shifted my weight on the porch, my jaw clenching until my teeth ached. I wanted to shout, to tell him that his need for demarcation was suffocating, but the words caught, lodging somewhere behind my ribs as a hard, unyielding stone.

[story 3]

I spent the entire morning sitting on my steps, nursing a lukewarm cup of tea. The rhythmic thudding from across the street was maddeningly consistent. I could hear the scrape of the shovel, the snap of the wire. It wasn't just noise; it was an imposition, a relentless declaration of ownership imposed upon my peace. When he finally called over, wiping sweat from his forehead with the back of a gloved hand, his tone was light, almost apologetic. "Sorry about the racket," he said, his voice too cheerful for the disruption he was causing. I simply looked down at my hands, noticing the faint tremor in my fingers. It felt like every ounce of my patience was being systematically eroded, chipped away by this ongoing, unavoidable project.
